lens.included_activities: Independent US firms performing repeat outsourced nanotechnology research and experimental development for external customers, including nanoscale materials and process research, experimental design, synthesis, characterization, prototyping, data analysis, and method development.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive corporate laboratories; university, nonprofit, and government laboratories; proprietary-technology or product developers funded mainly to develop their own intellectual property; grant-only researchers without recurring external customers; pure analytical testing classified outside 541713; manufacturers; shells; and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers are industrial, life-science, semiconductor, energy, government, and other organizations outsourcing defined R&D programs. Revenue is earned through project statements of work, fixed-fee or capped studies, milestone arrangements, fee-for-service or unit-rate work, and sometimes cost-reimbursable contracts; repeat business depends on specialized facilities, scientific credibility, confidentiality, and reproducible delivery.
lens.deviation_from_default: The NAICS definition includes both externally funded contract R&D organizations and firms developing proprietary processes, materials, or products with their own funds. Those models have different revenue, transfer, and value-capture economics, so the lens retains repeat client-funded contract R&D and excludes primarily self-funded proprietary development for coherence, not attractiveness.

a.bridge: BLS measures the wage and employment mix for four-digit scientific R&D services rather than six-digit nanotechnology contract R&D. I mapped partial exposure within management, business, computing, engineering, scientific, and technician work to literature search, computational screening, experiment planning, coding, image and spectral analysis, documentation, and project administration, then reduced exposure for physical sample preparation, instrument operation, safety, maintenance, and tacit scientific judgment. The broader BLS mix may overstate exposure if nanotechnology firms are more laboratory-intensive, while demonstrated autonomous nanomaterials experiments may make the high case understated.
a.rationale: The adjacent occupation mix is concentrated in scientists, engineers, computing staff, managers, and business staff, whose information-heavy tasks offer meaningful but partial AI substitution or avoided hiring. NIST documents machine-learning tools for instrument communication, data analysis, and autonomous campaigns, and NSF describes autonomous quantum-dot synthesis; neither eliminates the physical and accountable portions of client work. [S2, S5, S6]
a.change_evidence: A time-and-motion study from 15 to 25 independent nanotechnology contract laboratories, linked to payroll by occupation and identifying tasks already automated, would materially replace the task mapping.
caveats: The BLS occupation table covers NAICS 541700, including life-science and other R&D businesses with different workflows.
Autonomous-laboratory examples demonstrate technical possibility, not representative current adoption or labor savings.

The injected compensation-to-receipts ratio appears unusually low for laboratory R&D and may dilute labor opportunity because receipts can reflect funding or proprietary-value flows not comparable with ordinary service revenue.

rho.bridge: NIST and NSF report enabling tools and successful autonomous materials experiments in research settings, not five-year implementation rates in lower-middle-market contract nanotechnology laboratories. I translated demonstrated instrument integration, physics-informed modeling, and closed-loop experimentation into a moderate adoption case, then discounted for heterogeneous instruments, sparse proprietary data, validation, cybersecurity, customer acceptance, safety, capital needs, and limited implementation staff. Research demonstrations likely overstate near-term routine deployment, while modular open-source tooling may accelerate it.
rho.rationale: Implementation should advance first in bounded workflows such as document synthesis, analysis pipelines, experiment prioritization, and standardized instrument loops. Full autonomous execution will diffuse more slowly because client laboratories must preserve traceability, reproducibility, physical safety, and expert review. [S5, S6]
rho.change_evidence: A 2028 or later survey of independent materials and nanotechnology laboratories reporting production AI use by workflow, validation status, deployment cost, and measured hours saved would materially change this estimate.
caveats: The cited systems are leading-edge research programs rather than a representative sample of commercial laboratories.
Implementation depends on equipment interfaces and data quality, which vary substantially by nanoscale modality.
This range excludes pricing and customer pass-through, which are reflected in q.

e.bridge: NSF's 2015 survey covers nanotechnology-active companies in four-digit scientific R&D services, not current six-digit lower-middle-market firms or repeat-service eligibility. Of 229 nanotechnology companies in that sector, 137 reported some R&D paid by others, while externally paid R&D accounted for $304 million of $450 million total nanotechnology R&D. I use the company incidence as an anchor and haircut it because paid-by-others can include one-off grants and contracts rather than repeat customers; mixed firms with recurring client work but substantial company funding create possible understatement.
e.rationale: External funding is common but not universal, and the NAICS definition does not require a client-service model. A little over half of the estimated lower-middle-market population is therefore judged eligible after excluding primarily proprietary, captive, grant-only, and noncommercial operations. [S1, S3]
e.change_evidence: A current establishment-level census or vendor dataset classifying NAICS 541713 firms by customer revenue, grant revenue, proprietary-product revenue, independence, and normalized EBITDA would materially change eligibility.
caveats: The NSF data are from 2015 and use the broader NAICS 5417 sector.
Companies may appear in both company-funded and externally funded categories, and external funding does not prove recurring customers.

The injected firm count is a size-class and generic-margin estimate; R&D losses, grants, milestone revenue, and intellectual-property economics can materially misclassify firms into the target EBITDA band.

s5.bridge: Census reports that more than half of US business owners were at least 55 in the 2016 Annual Survey of Entrepreneurs, while PwC reports active 2026 transactions and platform formation in adjacent life-science services, tools, CRO, CDMO, and bioprocessing. I translate broad succession pressure plus adjacent strategic and private-equity demand into a five-year control-transfer probability for eligible nanotechnology contract laboratories. Broad owner age likely overstates founder succession for institutionally backed science firms, whereas adjacent deal activity likely overstates liquidity for small nanotechnology specialists.
s5.rationale: Aging private owners and buyer interest in specialized science services support nontrivial availability, but transaction evidence is adjacent and skewed toward larger life-science assets. Technical founder dependence, customer concentration, uncertain IP ownership, and modest scale suppress the base case. [S7, S9]
s5.change_evidence: Five years of PitchBook, GF Data, or Capital IQ transactions matched to a verified roster of independent NAICS 541713 contract laboratories, plus owner-age and succession interviews, would materially change the transfer probability.
caveats: The owner-age statistic covers all US employer businesses and is dated.
PwC's evidence is for the broader pharmaceutical and life-science ecosystem, not nanotechnology R&D specifically.
The injected lower-middle-market firm pool is uncertain because its margin bridge is not tailored to R&D economics.

q.bridge: Charles River Laboratories is a large adjacent contract-research provider, not a lower-middle-market nanotechnology laboratory. Its filing says many contracts are fixed-price or fee-for-service with a cap, placing cost-overrun risk on the provider, and also describes fixed-fee cost-to-cost and rate-per-unit or time-based arrangements. I infer that fixed and capped projects initially retain more implemented savings, while unit, hourly, reimbursable, renewal-priced, and competitively bid work shares them with customers. Large-provider contracting may have more pricing leverage than small specialists, biasing retention upward; niche expertise may offset that for scarce capabilities.
q.rationale: Mixed contract forms create moderate retention. Fixed-fee and capped statements of work let an operator keep delivery efficiencies during the contract, but renewals, transparent labor assumptions, government cost reimbursement, customer procurement, and competition progressively pass benefits through. [S8]
q.change_evidence: A sample of 100 recent statements of work from 15 to 25 nanotechnology contract laboratories, coded by fixed fee, cap, unit rate, hourly, cost-plus, repricing cadence, change orders, and realized gross margin, would materially change retention.
caveats: The proxy is a much larger preclinical and discovery-services company with a different service mix and bargaining position.
No public source quantifies the contract-type mix or renewal pass-through for NAICS 541713.
The range concerns commercial sharing of implemented gross benefit and does not duplicate implementation risk.

d5.bridge: BLS projects four-digit scientific R&D services real output to grow 2.0 percent annually from 2024 to 2034 and employment in physical, engineering, and life-science R&D to grow 0.8 percent annually. I compound the real-output rate over five years to anchor the base near 1.10, then widen for nanotechnology's narrower funding cycles, commercialization uncertainty, and exposure to semiconductor, energy, materials, and life-science investment. The broad sector can overstate demand if nanotechnology loses funding share or understate it if nanoscale applications diffuse faster.
d5.rationale: The current outsourced service basket should broadly track growing real scientific-R&D activity, but the six-digit niche is volatile enough to include modest contraction in the low case and materially faster growth in the high case. [S4]
d5.change_evidence: Current and historical six-digit NAICS 541713 constant-dollar receipts, customer R&D outsourcing budgets, and funded-project backlogs from a representative laboratory panel would materially change the demand ratio.
caveats: BLS publishes output for NAICS 541700, not 541713, and projections are not forecasts of cyclical funding shocks.
The estimate holds service quality constant and does not treat faster AI delivery as demand growth.
Government research priorities and capital-market cycles can move nanotechnology demand independently of broad scientific R&D.

o.bridge: The Census definition establishes a physical experimental-development service, while NIST and NSF demonstrate that software and autonomous systems can perform instrument communication, analysis, campaign design, and even closed-loop quantum-dot synthesis. Neither source measures the share of future outsourced quantity still requiring a contract-laboratory operator. I infer high operator requirement because customers still need facilities, materials handling, calibration, safety, traceability, validation, IP stewardship, and scientific accountability. Demonstrations may overstate displacement because they remain supervised research systems, but they may understate future self-service if integrated platforms become turnkey.
o.rationale: AI may remove stand-alone literature, routine analysis, and some experiment-planning work, and well-capitalized customers may internalize turnkey workflows. Most repeat outsourced nanotechnology R&D should nevertheless require an accountable laboratory operator to execute, validate, and defend physical results. [S1, S5, S6]
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement surveys and laboratory revenue cohorts separating work lost to software, autonomous equipment, customer insourcing, and alternative suppliers would materially change the operator-required share.
caveats: No source directly measures self-service substitution in commercial nanotechnology R&D.
Operator requirement varies sharply between computational design, standardized characterization, bespoke synthesis, and regulated nanobiotechnology.
The estimate allows AI to change how the operator works; it only counts quantity eliminated, self-served, or supplied entirely by software.

narrative.executive_view: The coherent opportunity is not all establishments labeled nanotechnology R&D, but independent laboratories repeatedly selling defined research programs to external customers. That segment combines information-rich scientific work that AI can accelerate with physical facilities and accountability that remain difficult to self-serve. The central uncertainty is population identity: public data do not cleanly separate contract laboratories from proprietary technology developers. [S1, S3]
narrative.ai_impact: AI can compress literature review, computational screening, experiment planning, coding, microscopy and spectral analysis, anomaly detection, documentation, and project administration. Closed-loop systems can also choose and run bounded materials experiments, but physical preparation, instrument reliability, safety, validation, and expert interpretation constrain substitution and five-year implementation. [S2, S5, S6]
narrative.value_capture: Contract form matters. Fixed-price and capped studies can preserve savings during an engagement, while hourly, unit-rate, cost-reimbursable, and transparently costed contracts transmit more benefit to the customer. Competitive rebids and renewals should share gains over time even when the first deployment expands margin. [S8]
narrative.firm_availability: Externally paid nanotechnology work is substantial in scientific R&D services, but many firms also fund their own development, so only part of the coded population fits a repeat-client service lens. Broad owner aging and active adjacent life-science-services dealmaking support some control transfers, yet founder dependence, IP complexity, customer concentration, and uncertain normalized earnings make the transferable pool hard to verify. [S3, S7, S9]
narrative.demand_durability: BLS projects continued real growth in broad scientific R&D services, supporting a modestly growing five-year demand base. Autonomous tools change delivery more readily than they remove the need for facilities and scientific accountability, though standardized analysis and characterization may migrate to customer self-service or integrated equipment platforms. [S4, S5, S6]
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest risks are classification and measurement error, old and broad external-funding evidence, a non-industry contract-pricing proxy, project and grant cyclicality, scientific failure, equipment and compliance capital needs, key-person dependence, customer concentration, disputed IP ownership, and rapid insourcing by large customers. The injected labor ratio and firm-count bridge are unusually fragile for firms mixing services, grants, milestones, and proprietary development.
narrative.principal_driver: AI can accelerate expensive scientific analysis and experimental iteration while customers still need specialized physical laboratories and accountable results.
narrative.principal_weakness: The six-digit code does not reveal which firms are truly recurring contract-service businesses, making eligibility and transferable-firm availability highly uncertain.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Manual: 541713 Research and Development in Nanotechnology
sources.supports: Defines the industry as nanotechnology research and experimental development at roughly 1 to 100 nanometers, potentially producing processes or prototypes, and distinguishes testing and manufacturing activities.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 541700
sources.supports: Provides the adjacent scientific-R&D occupation mix used to map wage-weighted task exposure, including management, business, computing, engineering, scientific, and technician roles.

sources.title: Business R&D and Innovation: 2015, Table 20
sources.supports: Reports nanotechnology R&D companies and spending by source of funds, including 229 scientific-R&D-services companies, 137 with R&D paid by others, and $304 million of $450 million paid by others.

sources.title: BLS Industry Employment and Output Projections, 2024-2034
sources.supports: Projects 2.0 percent annual real-output growth for scientific R&D services and 0.8 percent annual employment growth for physical, engineering, and life-science R&D.

sources.title: Hermes: Modular Open-Source Tools for Autonomous Materials Science Experimentation
sources.supports: Documents physics-informed machine learning, instrument communication, data analysis, FAIR data archiving, and autonomous research-campaign tools for materials science.

sources.title: Autonomous lab discovers best-in-class quantum dot in hours
sources.supports: Describes a closed-loop system that autonomously ran quantum-dot synthesis experiments and found a best-in-class solution in one day, illustrating technical feasibility rather than representative adoption.

sources.title: Business Owners' Ages: Over Half of U.S. Business Owners Were Age 55 and Over
sources.supports: Provides broad US employer-business owner-age evidence used only as a succession proxy.

sources.title: Charles River Laboratories International, Inc. 2023 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Describes adjacent contract-research pricing and revenue recognition, including fixed-price, capped fee-for-service, fixed-fee cost-to-cost, rate-per-unit, and time-based arrangements.

sources.title: Pharmaceutical and life sciences: US Deals 2026 midyear outlook
sources.supports: Reports active strategic and private-equity interest in adjacent life-science services and tools, including platform and bolt-on activity in CRO, CDMO, and bioprocessing.
